chunk_summary: Iron-folic acid supplementation and anemia treatment pathways are critical for addressing stunting and chronic undernutrition, with tailored dosing schedules for children, adolescents, and pregnant women in India’s anemia programs.
learning_objectives: Understand the role of iron-folic acid supplementation in stunting care and anemia prevention.
Learn the standardized dosing schedules for different age groups and physiological states in India’s anemia programs.
Identify strategies to improve adherence to iron supplementation, including practical counseling and addressing side effects.
key_facts: Prophylactic IFA dosing includes biweekly syrup (20 mg iron + 100 mcg folic acid) for children 6–59 months, weekly tablets (45 mg iron + 400 mcg folic acid) for children 5–10 years, and weekly tablets (60 mg iron + 500 mcg folic acid) for adolescents 10–19 years.
Pregnant women receive daily supplementation starting in the second trimester and continuing postpartum.
Therapeutic dosing for confirmed anemia requires alignment with local protocols and reassessment for non-adherence, blood loss, inflammation, or hemoglobinopathies.
Adolescent girls benefit from linking IFA adherence to fatigue, school performance, and menstrual symptoms to improve uptake.
Iron should be taken with food to reduce gastrointestinal side effects and separated from calcium supplements when both are prescribed.
reference_quote: The AMB schedule includes prophylactic IFA for children 6–59 months as biweekly syrup (commonly 1 mL containing 20 mg elemental iron plus 100 mcg folic acid), for children 5–10 years as a weekly tablet (45 mg iron plus 400 mcg folic acid), and for adolescents 10–19 years as a weekly tablet (60 mg iron plus 500 mcg folic acid).
document_key_topics: Iron-folic acid supplementation schedules for different age groups
Anemia treatment pathways and reassessment protocols
Strategies to improve adherence to supplementation
Integration of anemia programs into stunting care
